problems.title: Learn Unix: echo and newlines
problems.instructions:
In this lesson you will learn the very first Unix command most people see: `echo`.

`echo` prints its arguments to standard output, followed by a newline. For example:

```sh
echo "Hello"
```

prints:

```text
Hello
```

If you pass multiple words, they are separated by spaces:

```sh
echo "hello" "world"
```

prints:

```text
hello world
```

**Your task**: write a single `echo` command that prints exactly:

```text
Hello from Unix!
```

{hints: Remember to include quotes so the shell treats the phrase as one argument; you do not need any flags for echo here.}
problems.starterCode: # Print a friendly greeting using echo

problems.title: Learn Unix: commands vs arguments
problems.instructions:
Unix commands follow a simple pattern:

```text
command [options] [arguments...]
```

For example, this lists the contents of the current directory:

```sh
ls
```

Here `ls` is the **command** and there are no extra arguments. To list a specific directory, you pass the directory name as an argument:

```sh
ls /tmp
```

Now `ls` is still the command, and `/tmp` is an **argument**.

**Your task**: use `ls` with a relative path so that it lists the contents of the current directory's `.` entry explicitly.

We will not validate the exact filenames, only that you call `ls` correctly.

{hints: `ls .` lists the contents of the current directory explicitly; you don't need any options.}
problems.starterCode: # List files in the current directory using ls and an explicit argument

problems.title: Learn Unix: stdout vs stderr
problems.instructions:
Unix programs can write to **standard output** (stdout) and **standard error** (stderr).

- Normal results go to stdout
- Error messages often go to stderr

We can redirect them separately. For example:

```sh
ls no_such_file 1>out.txt 2>err.txt
```

sends normal listing to `out.txt` and error messages to `err.txt`.

**Your task**: run `ls` on a missing file called `missing.txt` such that **only** the error message is captured in a file named `errors.log` and nothing is printed to the terminal.

{hints: Redirect stdout to `/dev/null` and stderr to `errors.log` using `1>` and `2>`.}
problems.starterCode: # Capture only ls error output into errors.log

problems.title: Learn Unix: piping between commands
problems.instructions:
A **pipe** (`|`) connects the stdout of one command to the stdin of another.

Example:

```sh
echo "a
b
c" | wc -l
```

Here:

- `echo` writes three lines
- `wc -l` counts them
- The output is `3`

**Your task**: use `printf` (or `echo`) and `wc -l` with a pipe to print the number `2`, counting two lines of input you create yourself.

{hints: Use a single command line. Ensure your first command produces exactly two newline-terminated lines.}
problems.starterCode: # Use a pipe to feed 2 lines into wc -l

problems.title: Learn Unix: grep for matching lines
problems.instructions:
`grep` prints lines matching a pattern.

Example:

```sh
printf "apple
banana
apple pie
" | grep apple
```

Output:

```text
apple
apple pie
```

**Your task**: read from stdin and print only the lines containing the word `error` (case sensitive).

We will feed some log-like text into your command.

{hints: Use `grep 'error'` with no filename so it reads stdin.}

problems.title: Learn Unix: sort and uniq
problems.instructions:
`sort` orders lines, and `uniq` removes **adjacent** duplicates. Combined, they give unique sorted values:

```sh
echo "b
a
b" | sort | uniq
```

Output:

```text
a
b
```

**Your task**: read lines from stdin and print unique lines in sorted order.

{hints: Use a pipeline `sort | uniq`; you do not need extra options for this exercise.}
problems.starterCode: # Sort and deduplicate lines from stdin

problems.title: Learn Unix: head and tail
problems.instructions:
`head` shows the first N lines; `tail` shows the last N lines.

Examples:

```sh
head -n 3 file.txt
```

and

```sh
tail -n 2 file.txt
```

**Your task**: using only stdin (no filenames), print the **last 2 lines** of the input.

{hints: `tail -n 2` reads from stdin if you donâ€™t give it a file.}
problems.starterCode: # Print the last two lines of stdin

problems.title: Learn Unix: wc for counts
problems.instructions:
`wc` (word count) can count lines, words, and bytes.

- `wc -l` lines
- `wc -w` words
- `wc -c` bytes

Example:

```sh
echo "hello world" | wc -w
```

Output:

```text
2
```

**Your task**: count how many **words** arrive on stdin and print only the number.

{hints: `wc -w` prints a number and maybe spaces; you can pipe it to `awk '{print $1}'` or use shell substitution to trim.}
problems.starterCode: # Count words from stdin and print just the number

problems.title: Learn Unix: find basics
problems.instructions:
The `find` command walks directory trees and lets you filter by name, type, etc.

Example:

```sh
find . -name "*.txt"
```

prints all `.txt` files under the current directory.

**Your task**: use `find` to print the paths of all regular files (not directories) in the current directory tree.

{hints: Use `find . -type f`; you do not need to sort the output.}
problems.starterCode: # Find all regular files under the current directory

problems.title: Learn Unix: pipelines in practice
problems.instructions:
Unix is powerful because you can **compose** small tools.

Suppose you have a log file and want to know how many times the word `WARN` appears.

One approach:

```sh
cat app.log | grep WARN | wc -l
```

This:

1. Reads the file
2. Filters lines with `grep`
3. Counts them with `wc -l`

**Your task**: build a similar pipeline that reads from stdin and prints how many lines contain `WARN`.

{hints: You can skip the `cat` and let `grep` read stdin directly: `grep WARN | wc -l`.}
problems.starterCode: # Count how many lines in stdin contain WARN
